{{nihongo|'''Shimotsu-Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko'''|下海上国造|''shimotsu-unakami no kuni no miyatsuko'', ''shimotsu-unakami kokuzō''}} was a [[kuni no miyatsuko]] who ruled the eastern portion of [[Shimōsa Province]] under the later {{ill|ritsuryō provinces|ja|令制国}}, corresponding to the modern [[Chōshi, Chiba|Chōshi]], [[Asahi, Chiba|Asahi]], and {{ill|Katori District|ja|香取郡}} area of [[Chiba Prefecture]]. Also written as "Shimotsu-Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko" (下菟上国造).

== Description ==
According to the "Kokuzō Hongi" of the {{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}, during the reign of [[Emperor Ōjin]], Kuzuki-no-Atai, grandson of the ancestor of the [[Kami-tsu-Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko|上海上国造]], was appointed kuni no miyatsuko. The [[Kojiki]] gives {{ill|Takehiratori-no-Mikoto|ja|建比良鳥命}}, son of {{ill|Amenohohi|ja|アメノホヒ|lt=Ame-no-Hohi-no-Mikoto|lt_ja=天之菩卑能命}}, as the ancestor of the Shimotsu-Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko.<ref>The ''Kojiki'' records that Takehiratori-no-Mikoto was the ancestor of the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|出雲国造}}, the {{ill|Mazashi no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|武蔵国造|lt=Muzashi no Kuni no Miyatsuko|lt_ja=无邪志国造}}, the {{ill|Kami-tsu-Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|上海上国造|lt=Kami-tsu-Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko|lt_ja=上菟上国造}}, the Shimotsu-Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko, the {{ill|Ijimi no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|伊甚国造|lt=Ijimu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|lt_ja=伊自牟国造}}, the {{ill|Tsushima no Agata-nushi|ja|津島県直}}, the {{ill|Tōtōmi no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|遠江国造}}, and other lineages.</ref>

Within its territory lay the {{ill|Kuriyama River|ja|栗山川}} and {{ill|Tsubaki Lake|ja|椿海}} water systems, from which as many as eighty [[dugout canoe|dugout canoes]] (''marukibune'')—forty percent of all such canoes discovered in Japan—have been unearthed, and the kuni no miyatsuko is thought to have been a major power that controlled {{ill|waterborne transport|ja|水上交通}} on the {{ill|Katori Sea|ja|香取海}}. In the [[4th century|4th]] to [[5th centuries]], the {{ill|Shakushizuka Kofun|ja|しゃくし塚古墳}} and {{ill|Sannowakeme Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|三ノ分目大塚山古墳}} were constructed,<ref>In particular, the [[stone coffin]] of the Sannowakeme Ōtsukayama Kofun is a {{ill|nagamochi-shaped stone coffin|ja|長持形石棺}}, a type regarded as the coffin of a great king, indicating the high status of the person interred.</ref><ref>The only other examples of nagamochi-shaped stone coffins in the Kantō region, besides the Sannowakeme Ōtsukayama Kofun, are the {{ill|Takayanagi Chōshizuka Kofun|ja|高柳銚子塚古墳}} in [[Kisarazu, Chiba|Kisarazu]], Chiba, the {{ill|Ofujiyama Kofun|ja|お富士山古墳}} in [[Isesaki, Gunma|Isesaki]], [[Gunma Prefecture]], and the {{ill|Ōta Tenjinyama Kofun|ja|太田天神山古墳}} in {{ill|Ōta, Gunma|ja|太田市|lt=Ōta}} of the same prefecture.</ref> and some scholars argue that, together with the [[Kami-tsu-Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko|上海上国造]], there existed a "Great Unakami Province" (Dai-Unakami-no-Kuni) that controlled an area stretching from central Chiba Prefecture through [[Ibaraki Prefecture]], [[Saitama Prefecture]], and into [[Tokyo]]. In the [[6th century]] the {{ill|Shiroyama No. 1 Kofun (Katori)|ja|城山1号墳 (香取市)|lt=Shiroyama No. 1 Kofun}} and {{ill|Omaeonizuka Kofun|ja|御前鬼塚古墳}} were built, and during the [[7th century]] transition from the kuni no miyatsuko system to the [[ritsuryō]] system, the early temple {{ill|Kiuchi Abandoned Temple Site|ja|木内廃寺跡|lt=Kiuchi-haiji}} was established.

Because the territory of the {{ill|Muza no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|武社国造}} lay between this kuni no miyatsuko's territory and that of the Kami-tsu-Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko, the development of the kuni no miyatsuko system in Bōsō is thought to have proceeded in at least two stages: the "Great Unakami Province" was split into upper and lower portions by Muza Province, founded in the 6th century by a power advancing from the central court; the {{ill|Inba no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|印波国造}} pushed into the upper reaches of the Katori Sea; and following the establishment of the {{ill|Chiba no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|千葉国造}}, {{ill|Sōsa District|ja|匝瑳郡}} was founded, further dividing the territory.<ref>Though not mentioned in the "Kokuzō Hongi", the name "Chiba no Kuni no Miyatsuko Ōkisakibe-no-Atai Yoshihito" appears in the {{ill|Nihon Kōki|ja|日本後紀}}, and an article on the founding of Sōsa District appears in the {{ill|Shoku Nihon Kōki|ja|続日本後紀}}.</ref>

As for the kuni no miyatsuko lineage, in addition to the Kuzuki clan named in the "Kokuzō Hongi" (whose [[kabane]] was {{ill|Atai|ja|直 (姓)}}), the Osada-Hibumatsuri clan (also with the kabane of Atai) is known. {{ill|Osada-Hibumatsuri no Jingo|ja|他田日奉神護}}, who claimed descent from the Shimotsu-Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko, left the document "Osada-Hibumatsuribe-no-Atai Jingo-ge" among the {{ill|Shōsōin Documents|ja|正倉院文書}}, and Osada-Hibumatsuri no Tokutari composed a sakimori poem in Book 20 of the [[Man'yōshū]]: "At daybreak, in the dim light of dawn / the boat that rowed away / behind the shadow of the islands / I know not where it has gone." Furthermore, the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} records that on the 27th day of the first month of {{ill|Enryaku|ja|延暦}} 4 (785), Osada-Hibumatsuri no Tokutoji was granted the {{ill|outer rank|ja|外位|lt=outer}} {{ill|junior fifth rank, lower grade|ja|従五位下}}; and according to the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}} entry for the 19th day of the intercalary third month of {{ill|Ninna|ja|仁和}} 1 ([[885]]), Osada-Hibumatsuri no Haruoka, senior district magistrate of Unakami District, was granted outer junior fifth rank lower grade for the achievement of paying the common people's chō-yō taxes on their behalf. Additionally, [[Katori Jingū]] was the {{ill|tutelary shrine|ja|氏神|lt=ujigami}} of the Shimotsu-Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko, and there is a theory that the Osada-Hibumatsuri clan that held the post of kuni no miyatsuko was the original priestly lineage of Katori Jingū, and that the Katori clan was under their authority.

== Notes ==
{{Reflist}}

== References ==
* Katō Kenkichi et al., ''Nihon Kodaishi Chimei Jiten'' [Dictionary of Japanese Ancient History Place Names], Yuzankaku, 2007, ISBN 978-4-639-01995-4, p. 238.
* Ishii Susumu et al., ''Chiba-ken no Rekishi'' [History of Chiba Prefecture], Yamakawa Shuppansha, 2000, ISBN 4-634-32120-3, p. 30.

== See also ==
* {{ill|Ikazuchi Shrine (Asahi)|ja|雷神社 (旭市)}}

== External links ==
* [http://www.haniwakan.com/kenkyu/boso/simo.html Walking the Kofun of Bōsō: Kofun of Shimotsu-Unakami Province]

{{Japanese-history-stub}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:しもつうなかみのくにのみやつこ}}
[[Category:下総国]]
[[Category:千葉県の歴史]]
[[Category:Kuni no miyatsuko]]
[[ja:下海上国造]]
[[Category:Need translation]]
